content:
# Mark Chapter 16 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 41r–41v (PDF pages 82–83) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_016_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_016_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ When the Sabbath was past, Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James, and Salome bought spices, so that they might go and anoint him.

² Very early on the first day of the week, when the sun had risen, they went to the tomb.

³ They were saying to one another, "Who will roll away the stone for us from the entrance of the tomb?"

⁴ Looking up, they saw that the stone had been rolled back — it was very large.

⁵ Entering the tomb, they saw a young man sitting on the right side, dressed in a white robe, and they were alarmed.

⁶ He said to them, "Do not be alarmed. You seek Jesus of Nazareth, who was crucified. He has risen; he is not here. See the place where they laid him.

⁷ But go, tell his disciples and Peter that he is going before you to Galilee. There you will see him, just as he told you."

⁸ And they went out and fled from the tomb, for trembling and astonishment had seized them, and they said nothing to anyone, for they were afraid.

---

**[The Gospel of Mark ends here in Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus, the two oldest and most reliable manuscripts of the New Testament. Verses 9-20, the "Longer Ending of Mark," are absent from both witnesses. They appear in later manuscripts and are widely regarded by textual scholars as a second-century addition, not part of Mark's original composition. This translation follows the earliest manuscript evidence.]**

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

1. **The ending at 16:8 — unanimous agreement:** All three translators end at verse 8, following Sinaiticus and Vaticanus. There is no disagreement on this point. The manuscript evidence is decisive, and all three provide strong notes explaining the absence of 16:9-20.

2. **v. 5 — "young man" vs. "angel":** All three translators preserve Mark's "young man" (νεανίσκος) without identifying him as an angel. Mark's restraint is deliberate and characteristic. Matthew makes the angelic identification; Mark does not. The council preserves Mark's own language.

3. **v. 7 — "and Peter":** All three translators note the significance of Peter being named separately. PNEUMA calls these "two of the most gracious words in the New Testament." LOGOS calls it "grace compressed into two words." EMET notes it as "profoundly moving." The council agrees: this is one of the most theologically pregnant details in the Gospel.

4. **v. 8 — The ending:** EMET preserves the grammatical oddity ("for they were afraid" ending with γάρ). LOGOS notes the literary significance. PNEUMA calls it "the most daring, provocative ending in all of ancient literature." I note that all three translators approach this ending with appropriate awe. Mark's Gospel does not resolve. It confronts. The empty tomb is announced; the response is silence and fear. The reader must complete the story.

5. **The young man parallels (14:51-52 → 16:5):** EMET and LOGOS both note the verbal parallel between the young man who fled naked and the young man in white at the tomb. PNEUMA develops this into a literary reading: shame replaced by glory, flight replaced by proclamation. Whether Mark intends this connection or it is a coincidence of vocabulary, the parallel enriches the reading.

6. **Final note on the Gospel of Mark:** Mark's Gospel is the shortest, the most urgent, and the most unsettling of the four. It begins with a voice in the wilderness and ends with a silence at the tomb. Between these two edges stands the cross — and at the cross, a pagan soldier speaks the truth that the entire Gospel has been leading to: "Truly this man was the Son of God" (15:39). The translation council has worked to preserve Mark's raw, unpolished power in every verse.

No ⚠️ flagged disagreements for this chapter.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
